docs.title: baglab
docs.text: ROS2 rosbag data analysis library. Load, explore, and visualize rosbag data with MATLAB-like simplicity using pandas DataFrames.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Lazy loading — open a bag and read only the topics you need
  • Dot-access — navigate message fields like msg.twist.linear.x
  • Geometry — quaternion/euler conversion, distance, pose interpolation
  • Signal processing — lowpass filter, FFT, differentiation, integration
  • Diagnostics — topic rate, message gaps, latency chain analysis
  • Plotting — time-series, trajectory, step response, error band

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • ROS2 (Humble / Jazzy)

docs.title: baglab (Python only)
docs.text:
pip install baglab

For development:

git clone https://github.com/Kotakku/baglab.git
cd baglab
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: MCAP acceleration backend (optional)
docs.text:
Installing baglab_mcap_backend enables high-speed MCAP reading without ROS 2 dependency.

cd baglab
sudo apt install liblz4-dev libzstd-dev
pip install --no-build-isolation ./baglab_mcap_backend

Check whether the backend is available:

import baglab
print(baglab.has_mcap_backend())  # True / False

baglab works without the backend — it falls back to rosbags (pure Python).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
import baglab

# Load a rosbag (lazy loading)
bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag")

# List topics
print(bag.topics)
# {"/motor/angle": "sensor_msgs/msg/JointState", ...}

# Access topic data (loaded on first access)
twist_df = bag["/cmd_vel"]

# Dot-access for message fields
twist_df.msg.twist.linear.x         # Series
twist_df.msg.twist.linear.df        # DataFrame (columns: [x, y, z])

# Timestamps
t = baglab.stamp_to_sec(twist_df)                  # absolute [s]
t = baglab.stamp_to_sec(twist_df, relative=True)   # relative (starts at 0)

# Reindex by publication timestamp
twist_df = baglab.reindex_by_stamp(twist_df)

docs.title: Plot example
docs.text:
import baglab
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag")
twist_df = bag["/cmd_vel"]

t = baglab.stamp_to_sec(twist_df, relative=True)
vel = twist_df.msg.twist.linear.df

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, sharex=True)
for ax, axis_name in zip(axes, ["x", "y", "z"]):
    ax.plot(t, vel[axis_name])
    ax.set_ylabel(axis_name)
axes[-1].set_xlabel("time [s]")
plt.show()

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: https://kotakku.github.io/baglab/

docs.title: License
docs.text: Apache-2.0

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • ROS2 environment (Humble / Jazzy)

docs.title: From PyPI
docs.text: pip install baglab

docs.title: From source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Kotakku/baglab.git
cd baglab
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Load a rosbag
docs.text:
import baglab

bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag")
print(bag.topics)
# {"/motor/angle": "sensor_msgs/msg/JointState", ...}

docs.title: Access topic data
docs.text:
Topic data is loaded lazily — only when you access it:

twist_df = bag["/cmd_vel"]

docs.title: Dot-access for message fields
docs.text:
twist_df.msg.twist.linear.x         # pandas Series
twist_df.msg.twist.linear.df        # DataFrame with columns [x, y, z]

docs.title: Timestamps
docs.text:
t = baglab.stamp_to_sec(twist_df)                  # absolute time [s]
t = baglab.stamp_to_sec(twist_df, relative=True)   # relative time (starts at 0)

docs.title: Reindex by stamp
docs.text: twist_df = baglab.reindex_by_stamp(twist_df)

docs.title: Time slicing
docs.text: sliced = baglab.time_slice(twist_df, t_start=1.0, t_end=5.0)

docs.title: Plot example
docs.text:
import baglab
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag")
twist_df = bag["/cmd_vel"]

t = baglab.stamp_to_sec(twist_df, relative=True)
vel = twist_df.msg.twist.linear.df

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, sharex=True)
for ax, axis_name in zip(axes, ["x", "y", "z"]):
    ax.plot(t, vel[axis_name])
    ax.set_ylabel(axis_name)
axes[-1].set_xlabel("time [s]")
plt.show()

docs.title: baglab.analysis
docs.text: Control / signal analysis utilities.

docs.title: delay_estimate(cmd, response, t)
docs.text: Estimate dead time / transport delay using cross-correlation.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: cmd : pd.Series Command signal. response : pd.Series Response signal (same length as cmd). t : pd.Series Time in seconds (must be uniformly sampled, or approximately so).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float Estimated delay in seconds.

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: ImportError If scipy is not installed.

docs.title: stepinfo(actual, target, t)
docs.text: Compute step response metrics.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: actual : pd.Series Actual response signal. target : float | pd.Series Target / setpoint value. If a Series, the final value is used as the steady-state target. t : pd.Series Time in seconds.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, float] Dictionary with keys overshoot, settling_time, rise_time, and steady_state_error.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Logic follows MATLAB stepinfo / python-control step_info.

docs.title: tracking_error(actual, ref, t=None)
docs.text: Compute tracking error statistics.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: actual : pd.Series Actual values. ref : pd.Series Reference / command values (same length as actual). t : pd.Series | None, optional Time in seconds. Reserved for future time-weighted metrics.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict Dictionary with keys error (pd.Series), mean, max, rms, and std.

docs.title: baglab.diagnostics
docs.text: Diagnostics utilities for topic rate, delay, and gap analysis.

docs.title: latency_chain(timestamps_dict)
docs.text:
Visualize pipeline latency across multiple topics.

For each topic, computes rate statistics and relative timing.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: timestamps_dict : dict[str, pd.Series] Mapping of topic name to timestamp series (float seconds).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.DataFrame DataFrame with columns "topic", "mean_hz", "mean_dt", "std_dt", "min_dt", "max_dt", sorted by topic name.

docs.title: topic_delay(pub_stamp, recv_stamp)
docs.text: Compute publish-to-receive delay statistics.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: pub_stamp : pd.Series Publish timestamps as float seconds (header.stamp converted). recv_stamp : pd.Series Receive timestamps as float seconds (index converted).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, pd.Series | float] Dictionary with keys "delay" (per-message pd.Series), "mean", "max", "std", "median".

docs.title: message_gaps(timestamps, expected_rate)
docs.text:
Find gaps (missing messages) in a topic.

A gap is defined as a period longer than 1.5 * (1 / expected_rate).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: timestamps : pd.Series Timestamps as float seconds. expected_rate : float Expected publish rate in Hz.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.DataFrame DataFrame with columns "start", "end", "duration", "expected_count" for each detected gap.

docs.title: topic_rate(timestamps)
docs.text: Compute topic publish rate statistics.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: timestamps : pd.Series | pd.DatetimeIndex Timestamps as float seconds (e.g., from stamp_to_sec) or as a pd.DatetimeIndex.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, float | int] Dictionary with keys "mean_hz", "std_hz", "min_dt", "max_dt", "count", "duration".

docs.title: baglab.geometry
docs.text:
Geometry utilities for ROS2 message data in DataFrames.

Provides conversions and computations commonly needed when working with geometry_msgs types (Quaternion, Point, Pose, Twist, etc.) stored as DataFrames or :class:~baglab.io.FieldGroup objects.

docs.title: angle_diff(a, b)
docs.text: Shortest signed angle difference a - b, wrapped to [-pi, pi].

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: a, b : float | np.ndarray | pd.Series Angles in radians.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float | np.ndarray | pd.Series Same type as a.

docs.title: normalize_angle(angle)
docs.text: Normalize angle to [-pi, pi].

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: angle : float | np.ndarray | pd.Series Angle in radians.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float | np.ndarray | pd.Series Same type as input.

docs.title: unwrap(angle)
docs.text: Remove discontinuities in angle series (Series-aware np.unwrap).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: angle : pd.Series Angle in radians, possibly with ±pi jumps.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series

docs.title: align_time(df1, df2, time_col=None)
docs.text:
Align two DataFrames by time via linear interpolation.

The output DataFrames share the same time index (the union of both input indices, restricted to the overlapping time range). Numeric columns are interpolated linearly; non-numeric columns are forward-filled.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: df1, df2 : pd.DataFrame DataFrames to align. If time_col is None, the existing index (assumed to be a DatetimeIndex or numeric) is used. Otherwise the named column is used as the time axis. time_col : str | None Column name to use as time. When provided, this column is set as the index before interpolation.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: tuple[pd.DataFrame, pd.DataFrame] Aligned copies of df1 and df2.

docs.title: interp_pose(df, target_time, position_cols=('position.x', 'position.y'), orientation_cols=('orientation.x', 'orientation.y', 'orientation.z', 'orientation.w'))
docs.text:
Interpolate pose data at target times.

Position is linearly interpolated. Orientation is interpolated via Spherical Linear Interpolation (SLERP) of quaternions.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: df : pd.DataFrame Must have a numeric index (time in seconds) and contain the position and orientation columns. target_time : array-like Times at which to interpolate. position_cols : tuple[str, str] Column names for position x and y. orientation_cols : tuple[str, str, str, str] Column names for quaternion x, y, z, w.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.DataFrame Interpolated pose with columns matching position_cols and orientation_cols.

docs.title: match_by_time(t_query, t_source)
docs.text:
Find the nearest source index for each query time.

Uses binary search (np.searchsorted) for O(N log M) performance.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: t_query : pd.Series | ndarray Query times (need not be sorted). t_source : pd.Series | ndarray Source times (must be monotonically increasing).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: np.ndarray Integer indices into t_source, one per element in t_query.

docs.title: resample(df, dt, time_col=None)
docs.text: Resample a DataFrame at uniform time intervals.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: df : pd.DataFrame Input data. dt : float Desired time step in seconds. time_col : str | None Column name to use as time. If None, the index is used (must be numeric, e.g. from :func:~baglab.stamp_to_sec).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.DataFrame Resampled data with uniform time index.

docs.title: to_numpy_2d(fg)
docs.text: Convert FieldGroup with x, y fields to ndarray of shape (N, 2).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: fg : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain fields/attributes x, y. Scalar input returns shape (1, 2).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: np.ndarray Shape (N, 2) or (1, 2).

docs.title: to_numpy_3d(fg)
docs.text: Convert FieldGroup with x, y, z fields to ndarray of shape (N, 3).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: fg : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain fields/attributes x, y, z. Scalar input returns shape (1, 3).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: np.ndarray Shape (N, 3) or (1, 3).

docs.title: cumulative_distance(position)
docs.text: Cumulative travel distance from consecutive xy positions.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: position : FieldGroup | DataFrame Must contain fields x, y.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series Cumulative distance starting from 0.

docs.title: distance_2d(pos1, pos2)
docs.text: Euclidean distance between two positions in the xy-plane.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: pos1, pos2 : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain fields/attributes x, y.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float | pd.Series

docs.title: distance_3d(pos1, pos2)
docs.text: Euclidean distance between two positions in 3D.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: pos1, pos2 : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain fields/attributes x, y, z.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float | pd.Series

docs.title: to_xy(position)
docs.text: Extract x, y from a position/point.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: position : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain fields/attributes x, y.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, float] | pd.DataFrame {"x": …, "y": …} for scalar input, or DataFrame with columns x, y.

docs.title: to_xyz(position)
docs.text: Extract x, y, z from a position/point.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: position : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain fields/attributes x, y, z.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, float] | pd.DataFrame {"x": …, "y": …, "z": …} for scalar input, or DataFrame with columns x, y, z.

docs.title: pose_error(actual_pose, ref_pose, frame='xy')
docs.text:
Compute pose error between actual and reference poses.

Returns along-track, cross-track, and yaw errors. The decomposition axes depend on frame.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
actual_pose, ref_pose : FieldGroup | DataFrame | dict | object Pose data. Accepted formats:

- DataFrame with ``position.x``, ``position.y``,
  ``orientation.x/y/z/w`` (Pose format).
- DataFrame with ``x``, ``y``, ``yaw`` columns (xyyaw format).
- dict or object with ``x``, ``y``, ``yaw`` keys/attributes
  (scalar — returns dict).

frame : str Coordinate frame for along/cross decomposition:

- ``"xy"`` (default): world axes. along = dx, cross = dy.
- ``"ref_heading"``: reference yaw direction.
- ``"ref_path"``: tangent of the reference path (estimated from
  consecutive positions).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.DataFrame | dict[str, float] Columns/keys: along, cross, yaw.

docs.title: pose_to_xyyaw(pose)
docs.text: Extract x, y, yaw from a Pose.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
pose : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Accepted formats:

- DataFrame with ``position.x``, ``position.y``,
  ``orientation.x/y/z/w`` columns.
- FieldGroup pointing at a Pose level.
- ROS msg Pose object with ``position`` and ``orientation``
  attributes (returns dict).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, float] | pd.DataFrame {"x": …, "y": …, "yaw": …} for scalar input, or DataFrame with columns x, y, yaw.

docs.title: quat_to_rpy(orientation)
docs.text: Convert quaternion (x, y, z, w) to roll, pitch, yaw in radians.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: orientation : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain columns/fields/attributes x, y, z, w. Accepts a single ROS msg (returns dict) or a DataFrame/FieldGroup (returns DataFrame).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, float] | pd.DataFrame {"roll": …, "pitch": …, "yaw": …} for scalar input, or DataFrame with columns roll, pitch, yaw.

docs.title: quat_to_yaw(orientation)
docs.text: Convert quaternion (x, y, z, w) to yaw angle in radians.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: orientation : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain columns/fields/attributes x, y, z, w. Accepts a single ROS msg (returns float) or a DataFrame/FieldGroup (returns Series).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float | pd.Series Yaw angle in radians.

docs.title: rpy_to_quat(roll, pitch, yaw)
docs.text: Convert roll, pitch, yaw (radians) to quaternion.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: roll, pitch, yaw : float | pd.Series Euler angles in radians.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, float] | pd.DataFrame {"x": …, "y": …, "z": …, "w": …} for scalar input, or DataFrame with columns x, y, z, w.

docs.title: yaw_to_quat(yaw)
docs.text: Convert yaw angle (radians) to quaternion with zero roll and pitch.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: yaw : float | pd.Series Yaw angle in radians.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, float] | pd.DataFrame {"x": …, "y": …, "z": …, "w": …} for scalar input, or DataFrame with columns x, y, z, w.

docs.title: twist_to_speed(linear)
docs.text: Compute scalar speed from linear velocity (x, y, z).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: linear : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain fields/attributes x, y, z.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float | pd.Series Speed = sqrt(x² + y² + z²).

docs.title: twist_to_speed_2d(linear)
docs.text: Compute 2D scalar speed from linear velocity (x, y).

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: linear : FieldGroup | DataFrame | object Must contain fields/attributes x, y.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float | pd.Series Speed = sqrt(x² + y²).

docs.title: velocity_from_pose(position, time)
docs.text: Estimate scalar velocity by differentiating position over time.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: position : FieldGroup | DataFrame Must contain fields x, y. time : pd.Series Time in seconds (float).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series Velocity (distance / dt).

docs.title: yaw_rate_from_pose(orientation, time)
docs.text: Estimate yaw rate by differentiating yaw over time.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: orientation : FieldGroup | DataFrame Quaternion fields x, y, z, w. time : pd.Series Time in seconds (float).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series Yaw rate in rad/s.

docs.title: baglab.io
docs.text: I/O module for rosbag loading, field access, and timestamp utilities.

docs.title: FieldGroup
docs.text:
Intermediate node for chained ROS2-style field access.

Returned by :class:MsgAccessor attribute access. Supports further chaining until a leaf (single column) is reached, at which point a :class:~pandas.Series is returned.

Use the :attr:df property to obtain a :class:~pandas.DataFrame containing all columns under the current prefix, with the prefix stripped from column names.

docs.title: df property
docs.text: Return matching columns as a DataFrame with prefix stripped.

docs.title: MsgAccessor
docs.text:
ROS2-style dot access for DataFrame columns.

Registered as df.msg. Supports chained attribute access mirroring ROS2 message field hierarchy::

df.msg.twist.linear.x   # → Series
df.msg.twist.linear     # → FieldGroup
df.msg.twist.linear.df  # → DataFrame with columns [x, y, z]

docs.title: Bag
docs.text:
Rosbag handle with lazy per-topic DataFrame loading.

Use :func:baglab.load to create instances instead of calling this constructor directly — load expands shell paths, preloads topics, and selects the backend automatically.

Access topic data via subscript::

bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag")
df = bag["/cmd_vel"]                       # all fields, cached
df = bag["/cmd_vel", ["twist.linear.x"]]   # field selection, not cached

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: path : Path Path to the rosbag directory.

docs.title: topics property
docs.text: Return {topic_name: message_type} mapping.

docs.title: preload(topic_names)
docs.text:
Batch-preload topics into the in-memory cache.

On the mcap backend this uses a single-pass read through the file, which is much faster than reading topics one-by-one. Other backends fall back to sequential loading.

Preloaded topics are returned instantly by bag[topic].

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: topic_names : list[str] Topic names to preload.

docs.title: __getitem__(key)
docs.text:
Access topic data with optional field selection.

Usage::

bag["/motor/angle"]              # all fields, cached
bag["/motor/angle", ["actual"]]   # specific fields, not cached

docs.title: close()
docs.text: Close the underlying reader.

docs.title: explode_array(series, names=None)
docs.text:
Expand an array-valued Series into a DataFrame with one column per element.

Useful for sensor_msgs/JointState fields like position, velocity, and effort where each cell contains a list.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: series : pd.Series Series whose cells are lists or arrays of equal length. names : sequence of str, optional Column names for the resulting DataFrame. If None, columns are named 0, 1, 2, ....

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.DataFrame DataFrame with the same index as series.

docs.title: clear_cache(path)
docs.text: Remove the disk cache for a rosbag.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: path : str | Path Path to the rosbag directory. Shell notation (~, $VAR) is expanded.

docs.title: find_bags(pattern)
docs.text:
Return bag paths matching a glob pattern, sorted by data-file modification time.

Sorts by the modification time of the actual .db3 or .mcap files inside each bag directory (oldest first), ignoring cache or metadata timestamps.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: pattern : str Glob pattern (e.g. "/path/to/log_dir/*").

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[Path] Matched paths sorted ascending by data-file mtime. Use [-1] for the latest bag.

docs.title: has_mcap_backend()
docs.text: Return True if the baglab-mcap acceleration package is installed.

docs.title: load(path, topics=None, msg_paths=(), *, use_cache=True, backend='auto')
docs.text: Load a rosbag.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: path : str | Path Path to the rosbag directory. Shell notation (~, $VAR) is expanded. topics : list[str] | dict[str, list[str]] | None If a list of topic names, eagerly preload all fields for those topics in a single pass (fast batch read on mcap backend). If a dict {topic: [fields]}, eagerly load specified fields per topic. If None, return a lazy :class:Bag handle. msg_paths : Sequence[str | Path] Directories to scan for .msg files. Required for db3 bags that use custom message types not included in the standard ROS2 type definitions. Shell notation is expanded. use_cache : bool If True (default), use disk cache for loaded DataFrames. Set to False to always read from the bag file directly. backend : str Backend to use for reading: "auto" (default), "mcap", or "rosbags".

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: Bag A bag handle. When topics is provided, the requested DataFrames are pre-cached and accessible via bag[topic].

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Lazy loading (topics loaded on first access)::

bag = baglab.load("~/rosbags/experiment1")
df = bag["/cmd_vel"]

Eager batch preloading::

bag = baglab.load("~/rosbags/experiment1", topics=["/cmd_vel", "/odom"])

Field-selective loading::

bag = baglab.load("path", topics={"/imu": ["angular_velocity.x"]})

docs.title: align_origin(*series)
docs.text: Shift multiple time series so that the global minimum becomes 0.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: *series : pd.Series Time series in absolute seconds (e.g. from :func:stamp_to_sec or :func:recv_time_to_sec).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: tuple[pd.Series, ...] Each input series shifted by the same shared origin.

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: ValueError If no series are provided.

docs.title: find_time(series, condition)
docs.text: Return the relative time of the first element satisfying condition.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: series : pd.Series Series with a DatetimeIndex. condition : Callable[[pd.Series], pd.Series] A callable that accepts the Series values and returns a boolean Series. For example lambda x: x >= 0.5.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float Seconds from series.index[0] to the first matching element.

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: ValueError If no element satisfies the condition.

docs.title: recv_time_to_sec(df, relative=False)
docs.text: Convert the DataFrame DatetimeIndex (rosbag receive time) to float seconds.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: df : pd.DataFrame DataFrame with a DatetimeIndex produced by :func:load. relative : bool If True, return time relative to the first message (starting from 0).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series Receive timestamp in seconds as float64.

docs.title: reindex_by_stamp(df)
docs.text: Replace index with header.stamp as DatetimeIndex.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: df : pd.DataFrame DataFrame containing header.stamp.sec and header.stamp.nanosec columns.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.DataFrame New DataFrame with publish-time index. Original is not modified.

docs.title: stamp_to_sec(df, relative=False, stamp='header.stamp')
docs.text: Combine stamp sec and nanosec columns into float seconds.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: df : pd.DataFrame DataFrame containing {stamp}.sec and {stamp}.nanosec columns. relative : bool If True, return time relative to the first message (starting from 0). stamp : str Column prefix for the builtin_interfaces/Time field. Default "header.stamp".

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series Timestamp in seconds as float64.

docs.title: time_slice(df, t_start, t_end)
docs.text: Slice a DatetimeIndex DataFrame by relative time in seconds.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: df : pd.DataFrame DataFrame with a DatetimeIndex. t_start : float Start time in seconds relative to df.index[0]. t_end : float End time in seconds relative to df.index[0].

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.DataFrame Subset of df within the requested time window. The original DataFrame is not modified.

docs.title: register_msg_types(typestore, path)
docs.text:
Scan path for .msg files and register them in typestore.

The directory structure must follow the ROS2 convention::

<pkg_name>/msg/<MsgName>.msg

Types already present in typestore are silently skipped.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: typestore : Typestore The rosbags typestore to register types into. path : str | Path Root directory to search for .msg files.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: int Number of newly registered types.

docs.title: baglab.plot
docs.text: Plotting utilities for rosbag data analysis.

docs.title: plot_error_band(t, error, sigma=None, label=None, title=None, ax=None)
docs.text: Plot error time series with optional +/-sigma shaded band.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: t : pd.Series | np.ndarray Time axis. error : pd.Series | np.ndarray Error values. sigma : float | None Standard deviation for the band. Computed from error when None. label : str | None Label for the error line. title : str | None Axes title. ax : matplotlib.axes.Axes | None Axes to draw on. A new figure is created when None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: matplotlib.axes.Axes The axes with the plot.

docs.title: plot_step_response(t, actual, target, title=None, ax=None)
docs.text:
Plot step response with target line and settling band.

A horizontal line is drawn at the target value and a shaded region indicates the +/-2 % settling band around the target.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: t : pd.Series | np.ndarray Time axis. actual : pd.Series | np.ndarray Measured response. target : float | pd.Series | np.ndarray Target (setpoint) value. If scalar, a horizontal line is drawn. title : str | None Axes title. ax : matplotlib.axes.Axes | None Axes to draw on. A new figure is created when None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: matplotlib.axes.Axes The axes with the plot.

docs.title: plot_timeseries(t, *series, labels=None, ylabel=None, title=None, ax=None)
docs.text: Plot one or more time series on the same axes.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: t : pd.Series | np.ndarray Time axis. series : pd.Series | np.ndarray One or more data series to plot against t. labels : list[str] | None Legend labels. If provided, a legend is shown. ylabel : str | None Y-axis label. title : str | None Axes title. ax : matplotlib.axes.Axes | None Axes to draw on. A new figure is created when None*.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: matplotlib.axes.Axes The axes with the plot.

docs.title: plot_xy_trajectory(x, y, ref_x=None, ref_y=None, label='actual', ref_label='reference', title=None, ax=None)
docs.text: Plot a 2-D trajectory with optional reference path.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: x, y : pd.Series | np.ndarray Actual path coordinates. ref_x, ref_y : pd.Series | np.ndarray | None Optional reference path coordinates. label : str Label for the actual path. ref_label : str Label for the reference path. title : str | None Axes title. ax : matplotlib.axes.Axes | None Axes to draw on. A new figure is created when None.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: matplotlib.axes.Axes The axes with the plot.

docs.title: baglab.signal
docs.text: Signal processing utilities for time-series data.

docs.title: diff(x, t)
docs.text:
Numerical differentiation using finite differences.

Uses forward difference for interior points and backward difference for the last point. The first element is NaN because there is no preceding sample.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: x : pd.Series Signal values. t : pd.Series Time in seconds (same length as x).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series dx/dt, same length as x. First element is NaN.

docs.title: integrate(x, t)
docs.text: Cumulative trapezoidal integration.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: x : pd.Series Signal values. t : pd.Series Time in seconds (same length as x).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series Cumulative integral. First element is 0.0.

docs.title: lowpass(x, cutoff, fs, order=4)
docs.text: Apply a zero-phase Butterworth lowpass filter.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: x : pd.Series | np.ndarray Input signal. cutoff : float Cutoff frequency in Hz. fs : float Sampling frequency in Hz. order : int, optional Filter order (default 4).

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series | np.ndarray Filtered signal, same type as x.

docs.title: fft(x, fs)
docs.text: Compute the single-sided FFT magnitude spectrum.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: x : pd.Series | np.ndarray Input signal. fs : float Sampling frequency in Hz.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: freq : np.ndarray Positive frequency bins in Hz. amplitude : np.ndarray Magnitude spectrum (absolute value, normalised by N).

docs.title: moving_average(x, window)
docs.text: Centered moving average filter.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: x : pd.Series Input signal. window : int Number of points in the averaging window.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: pd.Series Smoothed signal with NaN at edges where the full window is not available (centered).

docs.title: baglab.stats
docs.text: Statistical utilities for signal analysis.

docs.title: describe(x)
docs.text: Summary statistics for a signal.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: x : pd.Series | np.ndarray Input signal.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dict[str, float] Dictionary with keys "mean", "std", "min", "max", "median", "p5", "p95", "rms".

docs.title: rms(x)
docs.text: Root Mean Square of a signal.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: x : pd.Series | np.ndarray Input signal.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: float Root mean square value: sqrt(mean(x**2)).

docs.title: Analysis
docs.text: The baglab.analysis module provides control-engineering analysis tools.

docs.title: Step response info
docs.text:
import baglab

info = baglab.stepinfo(response, t)
# Returns: rise_time, settling_time, overshoot, steady_state_value, ...

docs.title: Tracking error
docs.text: error = baglab.tracking_error(command, response, t)

docs.title: Dead-time estimation
docs.text: delay = baglab.delay_estimate(command, response, fs=100.0)

docs.title: Diagnostics
docs.text: The baglab.diagnostics module helps inspect topic timing and message delivery.

docs.title: Topic rate
docs.text:
import baglab

rate = baglab.topic_rate(df)
# Returns statistics about the publishing rate

docs.title: Message gaps
docs.text:
gaps = baglab.message_gaps(df, threshold=0.1)
# Detects periods where messages are missing

docs.title: Topic delay
docs.text:
delay = baglab.topic_delay(df)
# Measures delay between header.stamp and receive time

docs.title: Latency chain
docs.text:
chain = baglab.latency_chain(bag, ["/sensor", "/perception", "/planning", "/control"])
# Analyzes end-to-end latency through a processing pipeline

docs.title: Geometry
docs.text: The baglab.geometry module provides conversions and computations for geometry_msgs types stored in DataFrames.

docs.title: Quaternion / Euler conversion
docs.text:
import baglab

odom = bag["/odom"]
orientation = odom.msg.pose.pose.orientation

yaw = baglab.quat_to_yaw(orientation)           # pd.Series
rpy = baglab.quat_to_rpy(orientation)            # DataFrame [roll, pitch, yaw]
quat = baglab.rpy_to_quat(rpy)                   # DataFrame [x, y, z, w]
quat = baglab.yaw_to_quat(yaw)                   # DataFrame [x, y, z, w]

docs.title: Distance and trajectory
docs.text:
position = odom.msg.pose.pose.position

d = baglab.distance_2d(position)                 # Euclidean distance from origin
d = baglab.cumulative_distance(position)          # Cumulative path length

docs.title: Pose utilities
docs.text:
xyyaw = baglab.pose_to_xyyaw(odom.msg.pose.pose)   # DataFrame [x, y, yaw]

err = baglab.pose_error(actual, desired)             # DataFrame [x, y, yaw]

docs.title: Speed from twist / pose
docs.text:
speed = baglab.twist_to_speed(odom.msg.twist.twist)
speed_2d = baglab.twist_to_speed_2d(odom.msg.twist.twist)

vel = baglab.velocity_from_pose(position, t)
yaw_rate = baglab.yaw_rate_from_pose(orientation, t)

docs.title: Interpolation and alignment
docs.text:
# Interpolate pose at arbitrary timestamps
pose_interp = baglab.interp_pose(pose_df, target_times)

# Resample to uniform intervals
resampled = baglab.resample(df, dt=0.01)

# Align two DataFrames by timestamp
a_aligned, b_aligned = baglab.align_time(df_a, df_b)

# Match rows by nearest timestamp
matched = baglab.match_by_time(df_a, df_b, tolerance=0.05)

docs.title: Angle utilities
docs.text:
diff = baglab.angle_diff(theta1, theta2)         # Shortest angular difference
theta = baglab.normalize_angle(theta)             # Wrap to [-pi, pi]
theta = baglab.unwrap(theta)                      # Phase unwrap

docs.title: NumPy conversion
docs.text:
xy = baglab.to_xy(position)                      # ndarray (N, 2)
xyz = baglab.to_xyz(position)                     # ndarray (N, 3)
arr_2d = baglab.to_numpy_2d(position)             # ndarray (N, 2)
arr_3d = baglab.to_numpy_3d(position)             # ndarray (N, 3)

docs.title: Loading a rosbag
docs.text:
import baglab

bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag")

load() returns a Bag object. Topic data is loaded lazily — the actual deserialization happens when you first access a topic.

docs.title: Listing topics
docs.text:
print(bag.topics)
# {"/cmd_vel": "geometry_msgs/msg/Twist", "/odom": "nav_msgs/msg/Odometry", ...}

docs.title: Accessing topic data
docs.text:
df = bag["/cmd_vel"]

Each row corresponds to one message. Nested message fields are flattened into columns using dot-separated names (e.g. twist.linear.x).

docs.title: Dot-access with MsgAccessor
docs.text:
The .msg accessor lets you navigate fields using attribute access:

df.msg.twist.linear.x         # pd.Series
df.msg.twist.linear.df        # DataFrame with [x, y, z]

The FieldGroup object returned by intermediate access (e.g. df.msg.twist.linear) supports .df to get a DataFrame and direct attribute access for leaf fields.

docs.title: Timestamps
docs.text:
# Absolute time in seconds (float64)
t = baglab.stamp_to_sec(df)

# Relative time starting from 0
t = baglab.stamp_to_sec(df, relative=True)

# With custom stamp prefix
t = baglab.stamp_to_sec(df, prefix="header.stamp")

docs.title: Reindexing and slicing
docs.text:
# Replace index with publication timestamp
df = baglab.reindex_by_stamp(df)

# Slice by relative time
sliced = baglab.time_slice(df, t_start=1.0, t_end=5.0)

# Find index nearest to a given time
idx = baglab.find_time(df, 3.5)

docs.title: Backends
docs.text:
baglab ships with two reading backends:

Backend Formats ROS 2 dependency Speed mcap (baglab-mcap-backend) mcap None Fast rosbags (built-in) mcap, db3 None Slow

docs.title: Default behavior
docs.text:
With backend="auto" (the default), the backend is selected automatically:

  1. If baglab-mcap-backend is installed → mcap
  2. Otherwise → rosbags
# Automatic selection (recommended)
bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag")

# Explicit selection
bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag", backend="mcap")
bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag", backend="rosbags")

docs.title: mcap backend
docs.text:
A C++ backend specialized for the MCAP format. It uses the foxglove/mcap C++ library and a custom CDR deserializer to achieve fast reading without any ROS 2 dependency.

  • Zero-copy I/O via mmap
  • Parses ros2msg schemas embedded in MCAP files directly (no rosidl needed)
  • Self-contained CDR (Common Data Representation) deserializer
# Installation
sudo apt install liblz4-dev libzstd-dev
pip install --no-build-isolation ./baglab_mcap_backend

print(baglab.has_mcap_backend())  # True / False

Note

The mcap backend only supports the ros2msg schema encoding. Messages with ros2idl schemas will raise an error with a suggestion to switch to the rosbags backend. All standard ROS 2 messages use ros2msg, so this is rarely an issue in practice.

docs.title: rosbags backend
docs.text:
Uses the pure-Python rosbags library. It supports both mcap and db3 formats and requires no additional installation.

Use this backend when working with db3 bags or custom messages that only have ros2idl schema definitions.

docs.title: Preload (batch reading)
docs.text: When reading multiple topics, preload can significantly speed up loading. With the mcap backend, all requested topics are read in a single pass through the file.

docs.title: Via the topics argument of load()
docs.text:
# Pass a list of topic names for batch reading
bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag", topics=[
    "/sensing/imu/imu_data",
    "/vehicle/status/velocity_status",
    "/localization/twist_estimator/twist_with_covariance",
])

# Subsequent access returns instantly from cache
df = bag["/sensing/imu/imu_data"]

docs.title: Via bag.preload()
docs.text:
bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag")

# Batch-read topics once you know which ones you need
bag.preload(["/topic_a", "/topic_b", "/topic_c"])

# Preloaded topics return instantly; others are loaded on demand
df_a = bag["/topic_a"]       # instant (preloaded)
df_x = bag["/other_topic"]   # on-demand

Tip

Measured on a 20 GB bag with ~600 topics:

  • Sequential (bag[topic] x 100): 5.2 s
  • Preload (batch): 0.5 s (10x faster)

Use preload when working with many topics.

docs.title: Dict form (field selection)
docs.text:
The dict form is still supported. It loads only the specified fields per topic (does not use the single-pass batch scan).

bag = baglab.load("path/to/rosbag", topics={
    "/sensing/imu/imu_data": ["header.stamp.sec", "header.stamp.nanosec"],
})

docs.title: Custom message types (db3 bags)
docs.text: mcap bags embed .msg definitions inside the file, so custom types work out of the box. db3 (SQLite3) bags do not carry type definitions, so custom message types must be registered before the data can be deserialized.

docs.title: Option 1 — pass workspace paths to load()
docs.text:
The simplest approach. Pass the workspace src directory (or any directory tree containing <pkg>/msg/*.msg files) via the msg_paths argument:

import baglab

bag = baglab.load(
    "path/to/rosbag",
    msg_paths=["~/my_ws/src"],
)

Multiple paths can be specified:

bag = baglab.load(
    "path/to/rosbag",
    msg_paths=["~/my_ws/src", "~/custom_msgs_ws/src"],
)

docs.title: Option 2 — register_msg_types() for fine-grained control
docs.text:
If you need to register types into a specific typestore or reuse it across multiple bags, use register_msg_types() directly:

from rosbags.typesys import Stores, get_typestore
from baglab.io.typesys import register_msg_types

typestore = get_typestore(Stores.LATEST)
n = register_msg_types(typestore, "~/my_ws/src")
print(f"Registered {n} types")

Note

The directory must follow the standard ROS2 layout <package_name>/msg/<MsgName>.msg. Nested structures are resolved recursively, so pointing at a workspace src/ directory works.

docs.title: Plotting
docs.text:
The baglab.plot module provides ready-made plots for common visualizations.

All functions use matplotlib and return (fig, ax) tuples for further customization.

docs.title: Time series
docs.text:
import baglab

fig, ax = baglab.plot_timeseries(t, data, ylabel="velocity [m/s]")

docs.title: Trajectory
docs.text:
fig, ax = baglab.plot_xy_trajectory(x, y)
fig, ax = baglab.plot_xy_trajectory(x, y, color_by=speed)  # color by scalar

docs.title: Step response
docs.text: fig, ax = baglab.plot_step_response(t, command, response)

docs.title: Error band
docs.text: fig, ax = baglab.plot_error_band(t, error, label="tracking error [m]")

docs.title: Signal Processing
docs.text: The baglab.signal module provides common signal processing operations.

docs.title: Lowpass filter
docs.text:
import baglab

filtered = baglab.lowpass(series, cutoff=5.0, fs=100.0)
filtered = baglab.lowpass(series, cutoff=5.0, fs=100.0, order=6)

docs.title: Differentiation and integration
docs.text:
velocity = baglab.diff(position, t)
position = baglab.integrate(velocity, t)

docs.title: Moving average
docs.text: smoothed = baglab.moving_average(series, window=10)

docs.title: FFT
docs.text: freq, amplitude = baglab.fft(series, fs=100.0)

docs.title: RMS
docs.text:
import baglab

value = baglab.rms(series)

docs.title: Descriptive statistics
docs.text: stats = baglab.describe(series)
